sentences.text: My name is Miriam Freedman.
M-I-R-I-A-M.
Freedman-- F-R-E-E-D-M-A-N
Yes, my name was Eva Mannheimer.
And I will spell it to you-- E-V-A M-A-N-N-H-E-I-M-E-R.
Evitchka, ?] what is sort of affectionate name for Eva.
Yes, I was born second of seven-- 2nd of July, 1934, in Bratislava, Slovakia.
Well, my earliest memories are when we moved from our first home, what I believe was called Hochstrasse, to our second home.
And it was in [?
Suchamito ?] 12, in Bratislava.
I remember it was quite a commotion.
Nice thing was, we didn't have a car-- didn't come by car.
I think It was horse and cars.
I think I must have been about four or five.
And we lived in 12 [?
Suchamito ?] [INAUDIBLE] on a third-floor flat.
And the first impression was I was frightened to death.
I think our previous home must have been probably on a ground floor, but I don't remember.
But the fear of going three floor up was quite an experience, and I was quite frightened.
This was a strange experience.
Yes.
My mother and father-- my mother was a lovely, lovely lady.
She was rather introvert and quiet, very intellectual, interest in arts, and used to talk to lot of us.
But in those days, of course, I didn't appreciate it.
I was too young.
And she came from a small town called Nove Zamky in [INAUDIBLE].
And she was one of seven children.
Her name was Olga Mannheimer.
Olga Paskus was her maiden name, and her Hebrew name was Deborah Paskus.
No, she didn't come from Bratislava.
She came from a town called Nove Zamky-- not too far from Bratislava.
Well, I think when she was born, it must have been Austro-Hungarian.
And later, it was possibly one period, just Hungary and then Slovakia.
That's what I understand the historical period of that time.
My father's name was Solomon Mannheimer, and his family came from Piestany in Slovakia, and, I believe, originally came from Germany.
But I think our family lived approximately 300 years already in Slovakia.
He was in textile business.
No, I think he was working for somebody else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't really tell you awfully lot, but I do know that he used to travel around quite a bit, and he was away from home.
But that's what I was told, and that's what I remember.
Yes, I had one brother, and I had four sisters.
Yes, my eldest sister was Barbara.
We call her Bracha.
And my brother was [?
Martzie-- ?] Moshe.
My next sister was Edith [?
Tova, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then Naomi, and Gertie and then [?
Chava ?] at the present and myself, Miriam Eva.
I'm the youngest.
Oh, yes.
I remember quite a bit in Bratislava.
Well, first of all, I come from very orthodox family, strictly observant Jewish family.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the special occasions used to be on a weekend or summer holidays.
We used to go out.
Bratislava has got lovely hills and the parks, and we used to go out quite a lot as a family outing and picnics.
It was great fun on Saturdays.
And I remember going to school, of course.
To junior school, or kindergarten, you would call it.
And family gathering, not a lot, because we had an enormous big family, but cousins and uncles and aunt coming to visit us.
From my father's side, I remember only one uncle.
I think his name was Jeno.
But on my mother's side, we-- I used to see much more of them.
And we used to go, as I said, family outing and going to farm.
We had an aunt who had a farm, and we used to go there for summer holidays.
So we had great fun visiting the farm with animals and in the fields.
And my uncle and cousins, they had horse and carts, so we used to go out riding quite a bit.
Yes, we had quite pleasant experiences in the childhood.
You said your family were orthodox.
How orthodox was your father?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was he Hasidic, or--
No, I don't think my father was Hasidic.
I think he was rather on the traditional orthodoxy.
I don't recollect.
My father was clean shaven, and most of the men in our family, except for grandfather, are clean shaven.
I don't recall like Hasidic.
But I think he was very, very orthodox, in the sense that extremely observant and strict with all the dietary laws.
He used to go to Synagogue, probably twice a day if he could.
And I-- and he used to go yeshiva for shiurim-- for lessons-- and so did my late brother.
I cannot really classify how orthodox he was, but he was very orthodox-- observant.
No, we did not.
The custom used to be, in Bratislava, that there were some restaurants open on Saturday.
And you could pre-book, let's say, on Friday, tea or lunch.
And we used to go to those places.
It would have been like a guest house, but there was small restaurants.
I do remember going quite often to this kind of tea places.
So no, we didn't carry anything.
And actually, we had to wear long sleeves and socks.
That's how you can call, I don't know, ultra orthodox or very, very orthodox.
I can't put to the degree of orthodox in our family.
I don't recollect.
Our family was scattered quite a bit in different parts of Slovakia, and Hungary, as well.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one part of the family-- I think, my father maybe working, or my uncle was working with my father.
I can't tell you exactly.
We used to see them from time to time.
I do remember on Saturdays, afternoon teas.
My mother used to invite some friends or member of my family, but I reckon they might have been just friends.
I don't have very strong childhood memories of meeting many member of my family from my father's side.
On my mother's side, I seen-- we saw them a bit more, but then, they did not have children, except one of my auntie had a boy-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jeremiah [INAUDIBLE].
And I do not really-- and then, later I had another cousin from that side of the family.
But my father's side, we had been a very large family.

So presumably, it was difficult to organize family gathering, because our apartment was lovely where we lived, and I think it's three bedrooms, but I doubt that we could put up another six or 10 extra people.
I did not know.
No, I didn't.
Well, I think they passed away before I was born.
My grandfather-- well, I think he was in the grocery business.
And he had 13 children.
I think 12 survived.
And they lived, I think, in Piestany.
He was a scholar, a very well-learned man.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I don't know very much, as I never met him, and don't know very much about him.
Probably Mordechai.
I can't tell you.
Moshe Mordechai Mannheimer, because my brother name was the same.
And they may have called him after him.
I think her name was Gitel.
Or Pearl.
I may have it wrong.
I know that one of my grandfather was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Moshe Mordecai, and my grandmother may have been Pearl.
And the other side may have been Joseph and Gitel.
No.
I just heard some stories about them.
And very-- and now I'm regretting not those opportunity.
Well, I think they lived in-- in Nove Zamky [INAUDIBLE], so I presume they were from that area.
I haven't got much information, no.
Yes, we lived in Bratislava, and most of my mother's brothers and sisters lived in a town called Nitra, what was about 50 kilometer from Bratislava.
My mother was very close to her family.
Yes, I knew all member of my mother family.
Again, on holidays and visits.
I assume they may have been traveling, already, by train.
And I don't think that there was bus-- maybe there were buses already, but I think it was mainly by train.
One of my uncle had a car, so he was able to travel by car, as well.
I would say that, yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were poor afterwards, but at that stage-- at the beginning-- I would say very middle class family.
Yes.
I do.
I do remember going, in Bratislava, to a kindergarten.
I didn't like it terribly.
It was rather big, and it wasn't like today.
I think we used to sit in a circle and sort of-- some games and sing songs.
But it wasn't the sort of method that they use today-- [INAUDIBLE] and Montessori and all modern ideas to occupy children.
But I do remember going, but I don't think I felt very happy.
Yes.
About three hours.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My eldest sister used to take me, or any of my other sisters, and spent a few hours there playing.
I haven't got very strong memories and happy recollection of my kindergarten period.
My mother had plenty to do at home.
We are one of six children.
She didn't work out, though, and it wasn't so much a custom in those days.
She was a housewife.
She had to run a home and look after her family.
I think that's plenty to do.
Well, of course, things become disrupted after the war.
Things were not straightforward, of course.
Obviously, things were politically changing, and I did not know as a child.
But things were changing.
And I, obviously, moved to different area from Bratislava.
And in another district, we had to move out.
We went from that lovely flat.
We moved to another area called Judenstrasse.
And it was another sort of small place we lived in.
That was within Bratislava.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the schooling was disrupted.
I haven't got tremendous lot of memories of that stage.
From [INAUDIBLE] to-- I probably-- I must have been about four or five.
Yes, because we stayed there 1940, '41 in Bratislava before we moved to Nitra.
I reckon I was about six-- six, seven.
I don't have recollection.
I have got only impressions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think it was-- [? about ?] discussed with us the situation.
I think they tried to keep our family together the best way they could and some kind of normality.
Also, they knew that things are already bad, and I only found out much later when I was older.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was sort of a lot of commotion going-- coming and going-- and changes.
And they didn't explain to us why we have to move from one district to another.
But obviously, I knew afterwards-- the reason.
The last home in Bratislava that we lived-- I've got fairly strong memories, and it was not a very happy place.
We lived in a-- in one part of the Judenstrasse.
Obviously, it's-- Judenstrasse was the street of-- the Jewish street.
And we lived in kind of a cottage, and it was few rooms in it.
And we had shutters-- wooden shutters-- because many drunkards and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] young Hitler movement started to develop those days, and they used to throw bottles and so on.
So I remember that we used to go find a place quite early in the evening.
And we still had a maid, I remember, even in that place, because our maid was so well-trained by my late mother and father.
She used to always insist, before I eat, I wash my hands.
And she used a blessing and grace after meal and morning prayers.
She was well-trained.
I do remember that.
I think so.
I-- I would not know 100%, as I-- as I was the youngest of the family, but I think we probably had to go to live there.
Well, it wasn't a ghetto in such that there was gates, like in Venice.
But it was a ghetto in a sense that there was a curfew-- a certain time that we should not go out in the street.
I think we were slowly getting sort of into that setting, that Jews not as free to move around and-- or live in areas that they could live before.
Not that stage yet, no.
We-- we still had substantial orderly family life and food, yes.
I wasn't deprived of food.
Well, the school life was disrupted.
Because I know that I didn't go regularly to school.
I don't know exactly why I didn't go regularly to school.
I have such a vague memory of my early years in school that I remember only, in one class, that we were 60 pupils.
You can see in the photograph.
And we had a very strict regimental teacher who we had no any personal connection with her, except that I remember she was insisting that we sit orderly.
And she was walking up and down between the lane-- between the rows-- and with a ruler.
There was a blackboard.
And we learned writing.
And the writing has to be absolutely correct.
And I was left-handed, and she sis-- she insisted that I should be right-handed.
So she used to hit me quite often on my hand.
So as soon as I see her coming nearer to me, I used to switch my pencil or pen.
And so I was frightened of her.
This was a Jewish school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember this woman, somehow-- very stern, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very authoritative person.
And-- and I remember that I had to learn to write nicely and neatly-- and little bit arithmetic, as well.
Yes, I did, indeed.

Well, in our town, in Bratislava, that area, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people start walking with all swastikas-- youngsters-- all the disrupting people, in a sense that they came to disrupt us in a Synagogue service just to make commotions.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people used to disappear.
This I do remember.
My father used to come home and tell us that so and so disappeared.
We do not know where he or she is.
And some attractive women were disappearing, so they were afraid that they took them to do unpleasant work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shouting, throwing stones.
Generally, it was bit of a fear.
And we sort of huddled together.
All the Jewish people knew each other.
And it's a kind of feeling insecurity.
Actually, I do remember, as a five, six year old, certain amount of insecurity, people sort of not knowing where to do, where to go, so on.
I do remember this bit.
I did feel the fear, yes.
And then, one day, my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was it New Year, or it was Rosh Hashanah, or it was Yom Kippur.

I can't tell exactly, but there was a commotion coming one day that there is a lorry of Slovakian soldiers arrived-- and middle of the service-- disrupting the service, and all of the shuls of the prayer people, and told them to go on a lorry.
And they were taken away, and it was a terrible commotion and-- because, obviously, they're very religious people in the first.
So it was very important to observe the festival.
And that was the beginning.
And I saw that on one occasion.
And I ran home to tell my mother about it.
I used to go out, yes.
It wasn't such a big street.
I used-- yes, we could walk up and down, because matter of, like, the size of this street, [INAUDIBLE] avenue.
It wasn't a very large-- could've been bigger.
But the distance I used to go wasn't very far.
Yes.
My eldest sister wasn't there anymore.
She left in 1939 to Palestine, the [INAUDIBLE].
I think that was the last transport.
And I do remember that, with heavy heart, my parents said good bye to my sister who did advise to move, all of us.
She belonged to a movement called Maccabi Hatzair, and she went to Palestine.
So we were left now, five.
I can't tell you 100% if it was like that, but I presume that there was already rumors going that people around the age of 17 or 18 were sent to some labor camp to work-- the start of as a teenagers.
Or she belonged to the movement who knew something, and recommended, or told, the group to get out if they can-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or ideological reason.
There may be many reasons why she went.
But thank God she did the right move, because she survived the most terrible experience, what we have later on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, my brother was in a yeshiva.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was apple of my eye.
I really adored him.
I had only one brother.
And my other sisters weren't going to school as we always went to.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got on very nicely.
We had quite a nice relationship, all of us.
I presume between one or two years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, in 1941 or '40, all the Jews had to leave Bratislava.
Well, my father came one day at home, and I suppose he knew from the Synagogue or from the Jewish center that he used to go to, and his finances has been confiscated.
And so we had to move, fairly quickly, to another place, what presumably, my mother arranged with her sisters.
We went to Nitra, what was about 50 kilometers from Bratislava.
I do remember that journey, yes.
Yes, we had to sort of huddle up very quickly.
And I think there was some kind of lorry who took our stuff, our belongings-- our household goods.
And we moved to a place-- what I presume my mother must have arranged it with her sister, to a place-- a street called [INAUDIBLE] 28.
And that was a small town, Nitra.
Well, I don't-- I presume-- I don't think so, because the cottage-- the bungalow we moved to was quite small.
It was only a two-room one.
I think they put as much as they could in a table and beds and a few furniture-- as much as they could to squeeze into that little place.
And the rest, I suppose, my mother sold or left behind.
Do you know, it's a strange thing.
It-- it wasn't the Victorian period, but the times are so bad that children were seen but not heard.
So as a child, I don't think that I had very much opinion to express what I want.
I used to cry quite a bit and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] possibly wanted love and attention like any other child.
And I think our parents were very kind, very good people.
I mean, they gave us as much as they could, but I wasn't the only one.
Yes, I about was six or seven, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, we joined another Jewish school.
The Jewish area-- we didn't live in such a Jewish area, but there was sort of a small street.
The Jewish area was called the Vatican, strange enough.
There was a Jewish district where the Synagogue, the yeshiva was, and the butcher and slaughterhouses.
And that was-- Nitra was a very active Jewish community.
It had a reputation for a very good yeshiva.
I think so.
My mother had there three sisters [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a brother.
I think her other brother may have been living in another town, [INAUDIBLE].
And another sister of my mother was in England.
She was assistant in hospital here, and she came earlier out.
